tell.labMarker: No blood test; it's a clinical diagnosis. If imaged, ultrasound shows plantar fascia thickening (>4 mm at the heel; ~5–6 mm typical vs ~2.5 mm normal) and a hypoechoic origin.

fixes.what: Progressive plantar-fascia + calf loading (high-load heel raises with the toes extended over a rolled towel on a step), worked into tolerable discomfort
Supportive/cushioned footwear and arch support; avoid barefoot on hard floors, especially first thing in the morning
Cap sudden jumps in walking/running volume; build mileage gradually
Collagen peptides (~15 g) timed ~30–60 min before loading to supply building blocks during the load-driven synthesis window
Vitamin C alongside collagen — it's the required cofactor (prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase) for cross-linking collagen
Hit adequate daily protein (~1.6 g/kg) so repair isn't substrate-limited

causes.confusedWith: Heel fat-pad atrophy or a calcaneal stress fracture (deep, constant heel pain, tender on squeezing the whole heel), or Baxter's nerve entrapment — none respond to fascia loading.

causes.name: Chronic ankle instability (recurrent sprain)

chain.node: An inversion (rolling) sprain overstretches or tears the lateral ligaments, chiefly the ATFL (then CFL)
Ligament heals lax AND articular/muscle-spindle proprioceptors plus common peroneal nerve function are impaired
Impaired joint-position sense and slowed peroneal muscle reaction time — combined mechanical + functional instability
The ankle repeatedly 'gives way,' with recurrent lateral pain and swelling on uneven ground

tell.symptoms: A history of one or more ankle sprains; the ankle feels unstable or gives way on uneven ground; recurrent swelling on the outer ankle. Single-leg balance is noticeably wobblier on that side.
tell.labMarker: No lab; clinical anterior-drawer / talar-tilt laxity on exam, confirmable on stress imaging or MRI if needed.

fixes.what: Balance/proprioception training (single-leg stance progressing to wobble board / eyes-closed) — this rebuilds neuromuscular control, the part that actually breaks the re-injury cycle
Peroneal and calf strengthening to actively stabilise the joint
Brace or tape during cutting/court sports, and complete a graded return-to-play before full loading

causes.confusedWith: An osteochondral lesion of the talus or a peroneal tendon tear — persistent deep/lateral pain that balance work doesn't resolve; warrants imaging.

causes.name: Achilles tendinopathy

chain.node: Training-load spikes and a tight/weak calf repeatedly overload the heel cord
Repetitive microtrauma outpaces tenocyte repair; collagen disorganises and abnormal neovessels with accompanying nerve ingrowth appear
Degenerative tendinopathy of the mid-portion (2–6 cm above the heel) or the insertion — degenerative, not classically inflammatory
Stiff, painful heel cord, worst in the morning and when starting to run

tell.symptoms: Morning stiffness and pain in the tendon 2–6 cm above the heel (or at its insertion), a thickened/tender lump, and pain that flares at the start of a run then partly 'warms up.'
tell.labMarker: No blood test; ultrasound/MRI shows tendon thickening, hypoechoic degenerative change and Doppler neovascularity.

fixes.what: Heavy-slow or eccentric calf loading (Alfredson-style heel drops) — the evidence-based driver of tendon remodelling and neovessel regression; for insertional pain avoid dropping below level and use a small heel lift
Manage load: reduce sudden volume/hill/speed jumps
Collagen peptides + vitamin C timed ~30–60 min before loading to raise tendon collagen synthesis
Omega-3 to support the tissue's resolution/repair environment
Cut chronically high sugar intake (advanced glycation end-products stiffen and weaken tendon collagen)

causes.confusedWith: Retrocalcaneal bursitis or a partial/complete Achilles tear (sudden pop, marked weakness pushing off, positive Thompson test) — the tear is a red flag needing urgent assessment.

causes.name: Gout / crystal arthropathy

chain.node: Chronic hyperuricemia — from urate under-excretion (the majority) plus dietary load: high purine (organ meat/shellfish), alcohol (esp. beer), and fructose
Monosodium urate supersaturates and crystallises in a cool, distal joint — classically the big-toe (first MTP) or ankle
Crystals activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, driving an IL-1β cytokine surge and neutrophil influx
Sudden red, hot, exquisitely tender joint — often waking you overnight

tell.symptoms: Abrupt (often overnight) onset of a red, hot, swollen, extremely tender joint — classically the base of the big toe or the ankle; may follow alcohol or a rich meal; tends to recur.
tell.labMarker: Serum uric acid is often elevated; urate exceeds its solubility above ~6.8 mg/dL (~0.40 mmol/L), though it can read normal during an acute attack. Joint-fluid needle-shaped, negatively-birefringent crystals under polarised light are definitive.

fixes.what: Cut alcohol (especially beer), high-fructose/sugary drinks, and organ meats/shellfish; lose excess weight; hydrate well
Reduce added sugar — fructose metabolism consumes ATP and directly raises urate production
See a doctor for acute treatment (colchicine/NSAID/steroid) and, for recurrent attacks, urate-lowering therapy such as allopurinol treated to a target serum urate <6 mg/dL
Omega-3 may modestly dampen the inflammatory response

causes.confusedWith: Septic (infected) joint — also red, hot and swollen but a medical emergency; and pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate crystals, positively birefringent). A hot swollen joint with fever needs urgent assessment.

causes.name: Peripheral (metabolic/diabetic) neuropathy

chain.node: Chronic hyperglycemia / metabolic syndrome — high glycemic load, insulin resistance
Oxidative stress plus microvascular hypoxia and advanced glycation damage the small unmyelinated C and thinly-myelinated Aδ sensory fibres
Length-dependent distal small-fibre nerve degeneration (feet first, symmetric)
Burning, tingling, numb or stabbing pain in both feet, worse at night and unrelated to activity

tell.symptoms: Symmetric, both-feet burning/tingling/numbness in a 'stocking' pattern, worse at night, NOT triggered by a specific movement or step. Reduced sensation to touch or temperature.
tell.labMarker: Fasting glucose / HbA1c elevated (HbA1c ≥6.5% = diabetes; 5.7–6.4% = prediabetes); loss of 10 g monofilament and 128 Hz vibration sensation on foot exam (which flags already-advanced damage).

fixes.what: Tighten glycemic control — cut refined sugar and high-glycemic carbs, lose weight, and use regular exercise to restore insulin sensitivity. This halts/slows progression, but established small-fibre loss is largely irreversible, so acting early matters most
Correct contributing deficiencies (notably B12) and moderate alcohol, which independently damages nerves
Work with a doctor on glucose management if diabetic/prediabetic; alpha-lipoic acid has RCT evidence for symptomatic relief of diabetic neuropathy (not for reversing fibre loss)
Omega-3 shows emerging benefit for nerve-fibre health in diabetic neuropathy

causes.confusedWith: B12-deficiency neuropathy, alcohol-related neuropathy, or tarsal tunnel syndrome (usually one foot, tingling along the sole from posterior tibial nerve compression at the inner ankle).

sarcopenia.intro: Sarcopenia isn't one disease — it's several forces pulling muscle down at the same time, and which one dominates differs from person to person. In one person it's a chair-bound day with zero heavy loading; in another it's a toast-and-coffee breakfast that never crosses the higher per-meal protein threshold aging muscle now demands; in a third it's silent nerve loss stripping the fastest, most powerful fibers, or a smoldering inflammation from belly fat and chronic illness that eats muscle faster than food can rebuild it, or a vitamin D and testosterone/IGF-1 decline that quiets the build signal inside the cell. Most people over 60 have two or three of these running together. The job isn't to crown the 'real' cause — it's to find which ones are yours and pull each lever, because the fixes stack on top of each other.
ladder: Muscle mass and strength quietly shrink year after year
Breakdown starts to outpace synthesis — muscle stops 'hearing' the build signal
That signal fails from four directions: too little load, too little protein, aging nerves, and inflammation/hormone decline
Root levers: heavy resistance loading + enough per-meal protein (plus lowering inflammation and fixing a low vitamin D)
sarcopenia.theOneThing: Progressive resistance training 2-3x/week paired with 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day protein spread as 30-40 g per meal. Heavy loading plus enough per-meal leucine is the only intervention that simultaneously reverses disuse, overcomes anabolic resistance, and helps reinnervate surviving motor units — no pill or powder substitutes for it, they only amplify it.

causes.name: Physical inactivity & disuse (mechanical unloading)

chain.node: Sedentary days, no resistance loading, or a spell of bed rest/illness
Loss of load-driven mechanotransduction that normally switches on protein synthesis
FOXO-driven ubiquitin-proteasome proteolysis rises as synthesis falls
Fast-twitch (type II) fibers atrophy first
Strength and muscle size drop, fatigue on stairs

tell.symptoms: You sit most of the day and lift nothing heavy; strength drops faster than size, muscle feels soft, and you notice a clear step-down after any illness, injury, or hospital stay.
tell.labMarker: Grip-strength dynamometer below ~27 kg (men) / ~16 kg (women), or gait speed ≤0.8 m/s (EWGSOP2 cutoffs); DEXA appendicular lean mass index low (<7.0 kg/m² men, <5.5 kg/m² women).

fixes.what: Progressive resistance training 2-3x/week, all major muscle groups, taken close to failure
Break up sitting: daily walking plus loaded carries (rucking, groceries, farmer's walks)
Creatine monohydrate 3-5 g/day to amplify training strength and lean-mass gains
After any illness or surgery, get moving within days — don't let short disuse stack up

causes.confusedWith: Sarcopenic obesity — muscle loss hidden under normal or high body weight, so the scale looks fine while function declines.

causes.name: Anabolic resistance & inadequate protein/energy

chain.node: Low per-meal protein, low leucine, appetite decline ('anorexia of aging')
Increased splanchnic amino-acid extraction — less protein reaches muscle
Blunted mTORC1/p70S6K response to the leucine that does arrive (dose-response curve shifted right with age)
Post-meal muscle protein synthesis fails to exceed breakdown
Net muscle loss meal after meal, year after year

tell.symptoms: Your protein is skimpy and back-loaded — light breakfast, carb-heavy lunch, most protein at dinner. You fill up fast and often skip meals. Aging muscle needs a bigger per-meal dose to respond, and you're under it.
tell.labMarker: 24-h diet recall showing <1.0 g/kg/day protein (target 1.2-1.6); look for <30 g protein at breakfast and lunch.

fixes.what: Eat 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day protein split into 30-40 g doses across 3-4 meals — aging muscle needs the higher per-meal dose (~0.4 g/kg), not one big dinner
Anchor each meal with ~3 g leucine (older muscle's threshold sits higher than the young adult's ~2 g) — whey, eggs, dairy, meat, or fish
Whey or casein protein to hit the per-meal leucine threshold when whole food falls short
EAAs or HMB when appetite or total intake is low — HMB modestly protects muscle mass, though it doesn't reliably improve physical performance
Eat enough total calories — chronic undereating forces the body to catabolize muscle for fuel

causes.confusedWith: Malabsorption or undereating from GI disease, dental problems, or depression — the protein is 'eaten' but not absorbed or is simply too little overall.

causes.name: Neuromuscular junction degeneration & motor-unit loss

chain.node: Age-related death of alpha motor neurons (slow from ~60, accelerating with age)
Neuromuscular junction remodeling, fragmentation, and denervation
Incomplete reinnervation — orphaned fast fibers are lost or converted to slow
Motor-unit number falls, muscle shifts to a slow, weaker phenotype
Loss of power and speed, poor balance, falls

tell.symptoms: You lose power and speed out of proportion to size — hard to move fast, catch a stumble, or climb stairs quickly. Balance is worse, and you may notice cramps, twitches, or wobbly legs before size visibly drops.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; specialist EMG shows enlarged, fewer motor units (reduced motor-unit number estimation). Slow chair-rise and gait speed are practical proxies.

fixes.what: Heavy and explosive (power) resistance training to preserve type II fibers and promote reinnervation of surviving motor units
Adequate protein so the motor units you keep can rebuild maximally
Creatine monohydrate to support power output and high-velocity work
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) to support neuromuscular function and blunt anabolic resistance

causes.confusedWith: Peripheral neuropathy or motor-neuron disease (ALS) — rapid, asymmetric, or progressive weakness with wasting needs a neurology workup, not just training.

causes.name: Chronic low-grade inflammation ('inflammaging')

chain.node: Visceral fat, gut permeability, or a chronic disease (CKD, COPD, RA, heart failure, diabetes)
Persistently elevated TNF-alpha and IL-6
NF-kB drives the ubiquitin-proteasome atrophy program (MuRF1, atrogin-1) plus anabolic resistance
Myofibrillar protein breakdown accelerates faster than intake can replace
Muscle wastes despite seemingly adequate food and effort

tell.symptoms: Wasting alongside belly fat ('sarcopenic obesity'), persistent fatigue, and usually a known chronic condition. Muscle keeps slipping even when you eat well and train, because inflammation is overriding the build signal.
tell.labMarker: hs-CRP elevated (>3 mg/L), IL-6 elevated; often low albumin/prealbumin in advanced cases.

fixes.what: Resistance exercise — muscle contraction releases anti-inflammatory myokines that lower systemic cytokine tone
Reduce visceral fat with a whole-food, fiber-rich, lower-processed diet
Omega-3 EPA/DHA to lower cytokine signaling and improve the protein-synthesis response
Prioritize sleep — short/poor sleep raises IL-6 and cortisol, both catabolic
Treat the underlying chronic disease driving the inflammation with your physician

causes.confusedWith: True cachexia from cancer, advanced heart failure, or COPD — active involuntary weight loss with rapid wasting, a distinct and more aggressive syndrome than age-related sarcopenia.

causes.name: Low vitamin D & anabolic hormone decline

chain.node: Little sun exposure/intake, aging gonads, indoor lifestyle
Low 25(OH)D plus falling testosterone, GH, and IGF-1
Weakened VDR and androgen anabolic signaling inside the muscle cell
Lower protein synthesis and fewer activated satellite cells
Weakness, especially proximal legs (trouble rising from a chair)

tell.symptoms: Proximal leg weakness — hard to stand from a low chair or climb stairs — plus bone/muscle aches, low energy, and (in men) reduced libido. Often an indoor, low-sun lifestyle or older age.
tell.labMarker: Serum 25(OH)D <30 ng/mL (deficient <20); in symptomatic men, low morning total testosterone for age.

fixes.what: Vitamin D3 dosed to restore 25(OH)D to ~30-50 ng/mL, then retest — the strength benefit is real mainly when you start deficient (<20 ng/mL) and pair it with protein + resistance training; it does little in the already-replete
Resistance training — the strongest natural amplifier of anabolic signaling regardless of hormone level
Adequate protein plus calcium and magnesium cofactors
If clinically low testosterone with symptoms, evaluate TRT with a physician — don't self-treat

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism or statin-associated myopathy — both cause proximal weakness and aches and are reversible once identified, so check TSH and review medications.

ladder: The belly you can't shift is largely VISCERAL fat — packed around your organs and metabolically active, not just skin-deep padding
It expands when storage signals — insulin, cortisol, and shifting sex hormones — tell that specific depot to hoard fat and resist release
Those signals are set by how MUCH and WHAT you eat, how you sleep, your stress load, and your hormonal life stage
Root levers: protein + fiber to satiety, cut refined carbs and liquid sugar, lift weights, sleep 7-9h, lower chronic stress
visceral-fat.intro: The stubborn fat around your middle is mostly visceral fat — the depot wrapped around your liver and gut — and it behaves differently from the soft, pinchable fat elsewhere: it is more sensitive to hormonal signals and is often the first fat to grow when those signals go wrong. Here is the catch: it rarely has ONE cause. For one person it's a slow calorie surplus and too many refined carbs feeding fat straight into the liver; for another it's cortisol from chronic stress and short sleep parking fat preferentially at the belly; for a man in his 40s or a woman through menopause it's a sex-hormone shift that redistributes fat inward even with little change in diet. Most people have two or three of these running at once, in different proportions. The whole game is figuring out which are YOURS — because the fix for a hormone-driven belly is not the fix for a refined-carb belly, and chasing the wrong one is why generic advice stalls.
visceral-fat.theOneThing: For most people the single highest-leverage move is to eat protein and fiber to satiety while cutting refined carbs and liquid sugar, paired with 2-3 resistance sessions a week. Visceral fat is among the most metabolically active and lipolytically responsive depots, so it often mobilizes early on this combination — sometimes shrinking your waist before the scale moves much.

causes.name: Chronic energy surplus

chain.node: Sustained calorie intake exceeds expenditure
Elevated insulin drives fat storage and suppresses fat release (lipolysis)
Lipoprotein lipase loads triglycerides into adipocytes
Visceral and subcutaneous fat cells enlarge (hypertrophy)
Waistline creeps up alongside overall weight

tell.symptoms: Gain is spread across the whole body, not just the belly; waist rises in step with total weight; you can point to the cause (bigger portions, snacking, alcohol, sugary or liquid calories, eating out). Belly is often soft and pinchable rather than hard.
tell.labMarker: Waist-to-height ratio > 0.5 is the simplest flag; scale weight trending up over months confirms surplus.

fixes.what: Create a modest sustained deficit (~300-500 kcal/day); cut liquid calories (alcohol, sweet drinks) first
Anchor each meal with protein to satiety (~1.6-2.2 g/kg/day) to blunt appetite
Push dietary fiber toward 25-35 g/day to increase fullness and slow absorption
Resistance train 2-3x/week to preserve muscle so the deficit burns fat, not lean mass
GLP-1 agonist for significant excess weight — strongly reduces appetite and reduces visceral along with subcutaneous fat
Dual GIP/GLP-1 agonist — larger appetite and weight effect than GLP-1 alone, with a preferential pull on visceral fat
Caffeine or green tea extract as a modest thermogenic/appetite adjunct, not a primary lever

causes.confusedWith: Bloating and gut distension, which come and go with meals and are not fat gain

causes.name: Refined-carb / hyperinsulinemia-driven visceral & liver fat

chain.node: High intake of refined carbohydrate, sugar and fructose
Insulin switches on hepatic SREBP-1c while dietary sugar and fructose drive glucose/fructose-sensing ChREBP — together activating de novo lipogenesis
Liver makes new fat (de novo lipogenesis) and stores it locally and in visceral depot
Adipose insulin resistance raises free-fatty-acid flux back to the liver, self-reinforcing
Central belly fat and fatty liver, often with thinner limbs

tell.symptoms: Fat concentrated at the middle with relatively lean arms/legs; energy crashes and hunger 1-2h after high-carb meals; skin tags or darkened neck/armpit skin (acanthosis nigricans); family history of type 2 diabetes. Belly often feels firm, not pinchable.
tell.labMarker: Elevated fasting insulin and HOMA-IR (roughly > 2); high triglyceride-to-HDL ratio; raised ALT / fatty liver on ultrasound; fasting glucose and HbA1c drifting up.

fixes.what: Cut refined carbs, added sugar and sweetened/fructose drinks — the direct substrate for liver fat synthesis
Replace with protein and high-fiber whole foods to flatten the insulin response
Add zone-2 cardio plus resistance training — muscle contraction clears glucose without insulin
Berberine to improve insulin sensitivity and lower fasting glucose (AMPK activation)
Green tea extract as an adjunct for insulin sensitivity and modest fat oxidation

causes.confusedWith: Soft subcutaneous belly fat (pinchable, calorie-surplus type) versus firm visceral/insulin-resistant belly — press on it to tell them apart

causes.name: Sex-hormone shift (andropause in men / menopause in women)

chain.node: Age-related testosterone decline (men) or estradiol fall at menopause (women)
Loss of sex-hormone restraint on regional adipose lipid handling (LPL, lipolysis, lipogenic genes)
Fat redistributes from gluteofemoral/subcutaneous toward the visceral depot
In men, visceral fat's aromatase converts testosterone to estradiol, which suppresses LH and lowers testosterone further — a self-reinforcing loop
New central belly appears despite little change in diet

tell.symptoms: Belly showed up in your 40s-50s or across the menopause transition; fat visibly shifted from hips/thighs to waist. Men: low libido, fatigue, loss of morning erections, dropping strength. Women: hot flashes, cycle changes, sleep disruption.
tell.labMarker: Men: low total and free testosterone (below reference range), often with low SHBG. Women: low estradiol with rising FSH (postmenopausal FSH climbs well above premenopausal levels).

fixes.what: Resistance training — builds and preserves muscle and directly counters visceral redistribution (the post-exercise testosterone bump is real but transient and minor)
Lose visceral fat by any means to break the aromatase loop in men (fat lowers testosterone, which grows more fat)
Prioritize 7-9h sleep — a large share of daily testosterone is produced during sleep, and short sleep measurably lowers it
Menopausal hormone therapy reduces total and visceral adiposity in appropriate women — discuss with a clinician
Testosterone replacement for genuinely hypogonadal men (confirmed low labs + symptoms) under medical supervision

causes.confusedWith: Hypothyroidism, which also causes midlife weight gain and fatigue — check TSH before assuming it's sex hormones

causes.name: Cortisol / chronic-stress-driven storage

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress and HPA-axis activation
Visceral fat carries markedly higher glucocorticoid-receptor density and high 11β-HSD1, which regenerates active cortisol inside the cell
Glucocorticoid receptor activation switches on LPL, adipogenesis and glucose uptake with a preferential effect in visceral fat
Fat parks preferentially at the belly, amplified by stress-driven eating
Central weight gain 'even when I eat okay'

tell.symptoms: Gain concentrated at the belly despite a reasonable diet; high sustained stress; wired-but-tired feeling; cravings for sweet/salty comfort food under pressure; poor sleep. Belly tends to be firm.
tell.labMarker: No reliable routine blood test — random serum cortisol is unhelpful. If frank Cushing's is suspected (purple stretch marks, moon face, proximal muscle weakness), a clinician can order late-night salivary cortisol or a dexamethasone suppression test.

fixes.what: Attack the stressor and add daily downregulation (breathwork, walks, boundaries) — the only real fix for the driver
Protect sleep, which itself lowers evening cortisol
Ashwagandha to lower perceived stress and cortisol in stressed adults
Rhodiola rosea for stress resilience and fatigue
Magnesium to support the stress response and sleep quality
Front-load protein and fiber to blunt stress-eating swings

causes.confusedWith: Cushing's syndrome — rare but must be ruled out if there are purple abdominal striae, a moon face, easy bruising and proximal weakness

causes.name: Short sleep / circadian disruption

chain.node: Chronic short sleep (<6-7h), shift work, or late-night screens
Raised hunger and altered appetite hormones (higher ghrelin, disrupted leptin) plus higher evening cortisol
Resulting energy surplus is channeled preferentially into the visceral depot
A controlled 21-day sleep-restriction crossover (4h vs 9h) showed visceral fat rising ~11% while normal sleep held it flat

tell.symptoms: You routinely sleep under 6-7 hours; belly and weight gain track your worst-sleep stretches; strong next-day hunger and carb/fat cravings; late-night eating. Often overlaps with the cortisol driver.
tell.labMarker: No specific blood marker; a sleep tracker or a simple log showing chronic short/irregular sleep is the tell.

fixes.what: Extend sleep opportunity to 7-9h with a consistent wake time — the direct fix, shown to prevent the visceral gain
Cut late-evening screens, caffeine after early afternoon, and late heavy meals
Magnesium to support sleep onset and quality
Keep protein and fiber high the day after poor sleep to counter the hunger spike

causes.confusedWith: Undiagnosed obstructive sleep apnea, which both fragments sleep and independently worsens visceral fat and insulin resistance — screen if snoring, gasping, or daytime sleepiness are present

chronic-fatigue.intro: Fatigue is a final common exit for a dozen different upstream problems, which is exactly why it's so hard to fix: two people with identical exhaustion can have completely different machines breaking down.
ladder: You feel drained all day, no matter how much you rest
Your cells and brain aren't getting enough usable energy or oxygen delivered
One specific bottleneck is throttling it: unrefreshing sleep, low iron or B12 stores, blood-sugar swings, a sluggish thyroid, or run-down mitochondria
Which one is driving YOURS traces back to daily inputs: sleep timing, diet quality, nutrient stores, and movement
chronic-fatigue.theOneThing: Fix sleep first, before reaching for any supplement. Anchor a consistent wake time, get bright light within an hour of waking, and protect a 7-9 hour sleep window. It's the single biggest lever for most people, and it also acts as a diagnostic: once your sleep is genuinely solid for two weeks, any fatigue that remains points you to a testable driver (iron, B12, thyroid, or blood sugar) worth checking with bloodwork.

causes.name: Sleep debt & circadian disruption

chain.node: Short, irregular, or fragmented sleep; late-night light exposure
Blunted overnight adenosine clearance and shifted melatonin rhythm
Reduced slow-wave and REM restoration in the brain
Daytime sleepiness, low drive, brain fog, caffeine dependence

tell.symptoms: Energy improves markedly on weekends or holidays when you sleep in; you rely on caffeine to function; worst in the early afternoon 'dip'; you rarely wake feeling refreshed. Total sleep is under ~7h or is broken up.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; a wearable/sleep tracker showing <7h or low deep sleep is the signal. If you snore loudly and wake unrefreshed despite adequate hours, order a home sleep apnea test.

fixes.what: Anchor a fixed wake time and protect a 7-9h sleep window every day, including weekends
Get 10+ min of bright outdoor light within an hour of waking to set the circadian clock
Cut caffeine after early afternoon (~2pm) — its half-life is ~5-6h, so it keeps blocking adenosine into the evening
Magnesium glycinate in the evening to support sleep onset and depth

causes.confusedWith: Obstructive sleep apnea — loud snoring and unrefreshing sleep despite 8h in bed; needs a sleep study, not just better sleep hygiene.

causes.name: Iron deficiency (with or without anemia)

chain.node: Heavy menstrual loss, low dietary iron, or poor gut absorption
Depleted ferritin stores starve hemoglobin and iron-dependent mitochondrial enzymes
Less oxygen delivered to tissue plus impaired electron transport in muscle
Fatigue, breathlessness on exertion, pallor, restless legs

tell.symptoms: Breathless or heart-pounding climbing stairs, pale skin and inner eyelids, cold hands, hair shedding, restless legs at night, ice cravings. Common if you have heavy periods or eat little red meat.
tell.labMarker: Ferritin: <30 ng/mL is diagnostic of iron deficiency; three RCTs show iron helps fatigue in non-anemic women even up to ~50 ng/mL, where absorption normalizes. Confirm alongside a CBC (hemoglobin) and transferrin saturation. Note: ferritin can be falsely raised by inflammation, so check a CRP if it looks normal but you're symptomatic.

fixes.what: Eat iron-rich foods: red meat, liver, shellfish, lentils and beans, dark leafy greens
Supplement iron — alternate-day dosing gives higher fractional absorption (lower hepcidin) and is gentler on the gut
Take vitamin C with iron-containing meals to boost non-heme absorption
Avoid tea, coffee, and calcium supplements within an hour of iron — they block uptake

causes.confusedWith: Anemia of inflammation (chronic illness), which lowers hemoglobin but keeps or raises ferritin — iron supplements won't help and may harm.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings & insulin resistance

chain.node: Refined-carb meals low in fiber and protein
Glucose spike triggers an exaggerated, overshooting insulin surge
Reactive glucose dip and high glycemic variability (usually a relative drop, not documented hypoglycemia)
Post-meal crash: sleepy, foggy, sometimes shaky, hungry again within hours

tell.symptoms: Energy nosedives 1-3 hours after carb-heavy or sugary meals; a classic afternoon slump after a starchy lunch; feel shaky, foggy or ravenous between meals; steadier on higher-protein, lower-carb days.
tell.labMarker: Fasting glucose and HbA1c drifting toward the prediabetic range (HbA1c 5.7-6.4%); elevated fasting insulin or HOMA-IR. A CGM showing sharp spikes followed by dips is the clearest signal. True reactive hypoglycemia requires a documented glucose <70 mg/dL during symptoms; many people have the symptom pattern without that ('idiopathic postprandial syndrome').

fixes.what: Eat protein and fiber before or instead of refined carbs; cut sugary drinks and snacks
Take a 10-15 min walk after meals to blunt the glucose spike
Alpha-lipoic acid may modestly support insulin sensitivity and glucose handling
Magnesium — low levels track with insulin resistance

causes.confusedWith: Simple postprandial somnolence — a normal, mild 'food coma' from any large meal that doesn't come with shakiness or repeated between-meal crashes.

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (overt; subclinical is weaker)

chain.node: Autoimmune thyroid damage (Hashimoto's), or iodine/selenium inadequacy
Falling thyroid hormone drives TSH up; less active T3 reaches tissues
Slowed cellular metabolic rate and reduced Na/K-ATPase activity everywhere
Fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, sluggish mood and bowels

tell.symptoms: Cold when others aren't, dry skin, constipation, unexplained weight creep, thinning of the outer third of the eyebrows, puffiness, low mood, slow heart rate.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated: overt hypothyroidism is TSH >10 mIU/L with low free T4; subclinical is TSH ~4.5-10 with normal free T4. Add TPO antibodies for Hashimoto's. Caveat: for subclinical disease, RCTs show levothyroxine often does NOT relieve fatigue better than placebo, so don't assume a mildly high TSH is the cause.

fixes.what: Get a full thyroid panel (TSH, free T4, TPO antibodies) — this is a diagnosis you can't fix blind
Levothyroxine replacement for overt hypothyroidism, or symptomatic subclinical cases after shared decision-making (physician-guided)
Ensure adequate selenium and iodine — Brazil nuts, seafood, eggs, dairy

causes.confusedWith: Depression and anemia, which share the fatigue-plus-low-mood picture; a TSH test separates them.

causes.name: Vitamin B12 (or folate) deficiency

chain.node: Vegetarian/vegan diet, age-related low stomach acid, pernicious anemia, or long-term metformin/PPI use
Impaired B12-dependent DNA synthesis and methylation (methylmalonic acid and homocysteine rise)
Impaired red-cell production (macrocytic anemia) and faltering nerve-myelin maintenance
Fatigue, brain fog, tingling/numbness in hands and feet, sore smooth tongue, unsteadiness

tell.symptoms: Fatigue with tingling or numbness in the hands/feet, a sore or smooth red tongue, balance or memory problems; more likely if you're vegan/vegetarian, over 60, or on metformin or a long-term acid blocker (PPI).
tell.labMarker: Serum B12 low or low-normal; because serum B12 is unreliable, confirm with methylmalonic acid (MMA) and homocysteine — both rise in true deficiency. Check folate and a CBC (macrocytosis/high MCV) alongside.

fixes.what: B12-rich foods: meat, fish, eggs, dairy; fortified foods or a supplement if plant-based
B12 supplement — high-dose oral works for most; injections if pernicious anemia or malabsorption
If on metformin or a PPI long-term, ask for periodic B12 testing
Cover folate too (leafy greens, legumes) — but don't megadose folate blind, as it can mask a B12 deficiency

causes.confusedWith: Iron-deficiency and folate-deficiency anemia — overlapping fatigue; MMA/homocysteine and the blood-film indices (macro- vs microcytic) tell them apart.

causes.name: Mitochondrial inefficiency & cofactor depletion

chain.node: Aging, statin use, oxidative stress, or depleted CoQ10 / B-vitamin cofactors
Impaired electron transport chain lowers ATP output per unit of fuel
Reduced ATP supply in high-demand muscle and brain tissue
Still drained despite good sleep and normal iron/B12/thyroid; poor stamina and slow recovery

tell.symptoms: You've ruled out the obvious — sleep is good, iron/B12/thyroid/blood sugar all normal — yet you're still low on energy, with exercise intolerance and slow recovery after exertion. Often worse on a statin.
tell.labMarker: Standard labs are typically normal — this is largely a diagnosis of exclusion, and 'mitochondrial inefficiency' in an otherwise-healthy person is an inference, not a measured finding. In genuine mitochondrial disease, blood lactate may rise abnormally with modest exertion.

fixes.what: CoQ10 / ubiquinol — statins and age do lower circulating CoQ10, but RCTs of supplementation for symptoms are mixed and it isn't guideline-recommended; low-risk to trial for a few weeks
Creatine to buffer cellular ATP in muscle and brain
PQQ and alpha-lipoic acid may support mitochondrial function and reduce oxidative stress (evidence largely preclinical)
Zone-2 aerobic exercise — the strongest well-established stimulus to build new mitochondria

causes.confusedWith: Simple deconditioning — real, fixable fatigue from being sedentary, which also responds to graded aerobic training.

cravings.intro: A sugar craving is almost never about weak willpower — it's a signal, and the signal has several possible sources that differ from person to person. For one person the driver is a glucose spike-and-crash that leaves the brain convinced it's running low on fuel two hours after lunch; for another it's a single short night that pushed hunger hormones out of balance; for another it's cortisol from a stressful week pulling the reward system toward anything sweet; for another it's simply eating too little protein, so appetite stays switched on until the body gets what it's hunting for; and for many women it's the predictable premenstrual week, when appetite genuinely rises with the hormonal shift. Most people have two or three of these stacked on top of each other. The job here is to find YOURS — the pattern below that matches when, and why, your cravings actually hit.
ladder: You crave sugar or can't stop snacking a few hours after eating
Your brain is receiving a false 'need fuel' or 'need reward' signal
Driven by glucose crashes, high ghrelin / low leptin, stress cortisol, or unmet protein — and, cyclically, premenstrual hormone shifts
Rooted in how you build meals, how you sleep, your stress load, and cycle timing — nearly all modifiable
cravings.theOneThing: Rebuild your first two meals of the day around 30–40g of protein plus real fiber, eaten before any refined carbs. This single change flattens the glucose spike-and-crash that drives most afternoon cravings, satisfies the protein appetite that keeps hunger switched on, and gives a stable base so that sleep, stress and cycle drivers have far less to pull on.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings (reactive glucose dips)

chain.node: High-glycemic, low-fiber, low-protein meal
Rapid glucose spike followed by an insulin response
Glucose dips below fasting baseline ~1.5–3h later (reactive dip)
Counter-regulatory adrenaline/glucagon + hypothalamic hunger drive fire
Craving for fast carbs, shakiness, energy crash, irritability

tell.symptoms: Cravings and hunger arrive 2–4h after a carby meal, often with shakiness, irritability or an energy slump; a few bites of sugar fix it within minutes. Worse after breakfasts of cereal/toast/juice than after eggs.
tell.labMarker: On CGM, glucose rises sharply after high-GI meals then dips below fasting baseline around 1.5–3h; in the PREDICT-1 study these 'dips' predicted greater hunger and next-meal intake. Note dips also occur in metabolically healthy people, so a dip alone does not prove insulin resistance — but elevated fasting insulin / raised HOMA-IR marks insulin resistance as an amplifier when present.

fixes.what: Build meals protein-and-fiber-first, carbs last, to blunt the spike and the rebound dip
Front-load 25–40g protein and 8–12g fiber per meal
Berberine to improve insulin sensitivity and flatten post-meal glucose (meta-analyses show ~1.5 mmol/L lower 2h glucose and reduced HOMA-IR)
Myo-inositol to improve insulin signaling (esp. PCOS/insulin resistance)
Magnesium — repletion supports insulin sensitivity if deficient
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) for refractory cravings with insulin resistance/obesity — slows gastric emptying, blunts glucose swings and appetite

causes.confusedWith: True organic hypoglycemia (rare — e.g. insulinoma) or early/undiagnosed diabetes; get glucose formally checked if dips are severe or occur while fasted.

chain.node: Short or fragmented sleep (<7h)
Ghrelin (hunger hormone) rises
Leptin (satiety hormone) falls — weaker 'I'm full' signal
Endocannabinoid (2-AG) tone and reward sensitivity to calorie-dense food rise
Increased hunger, heightened sweet-taste preference, evening/late-night snacking

tell.symptoms: Cravings are markedly worse the day after a poor or short night, skew toward sweets and starchy comfort food, and hit hardest in the evening. Good sleep = the cravings largely disappear.
tell.labMarker: No routine clinical lab; confirm by tracking sleep duration against next-day craving intensity. Randomized crossover trials (Spiegel; Hanlon) show acute sleep loss raises ghrelin ~28%, lowers leptin ~18%, amplifies the afternoon 2-AG endocannabinoid rhythm, and increases sweet preference and intake.

fixes.what: Protect a consistent 7–9h sleep window with a fixed wake time
Cut caffeine after early afternoon and dim bright/blue light in the evening
Protein-rich breakfast to counter the next-day hunger-hormone shift
Magnesium in the evening to support sleep quality

causes.name: Chronic stress (cortisol-driven hedonic eating)

chain.node: Chronic psychological stress / poor stress recovery
Cortisol reactivity and sustained elevation accompany a pull toward quick energy
Reward system shifts toward sugar/fat while prefrontal self-control is impaired
Comfort-eating; sugar transiently blunts the stress signal, reinforcing the loop
Emotional, non-hunger sugar cravings, especially after stressful days

tell.symptoms: Cravings track emotion and stress rather than physical hunger, target specific 'comfort' foods, and spike in the evening after a demanding day. You eat when tense/tired, not when your stomach is empty.
tell.labMarker: No diagnostic test. A flattened or elevated diurnal/late-evening salivary cortisol can corroborate a high stress load, but the cortisol→craving link in humans is largely behavioral/associative (with support from acute stress-induction studies) — diagnosis is by pattern, not a lab.

fixes.what: Insert a non-food stress down-regulation habit at the usual craving time (walk, breathwork, short exercise)
Keep protein + fiber at each meal so stress cravings don't land on an empty, glucose-swinging tank
Prioritize sleep — sleep debt and cortisol amplify each other
Magnesium — depleted by chronic stress and supports HPA-axis regulation

causes.confusedWith: Binge-eating disorder — if eating feels out of control with distress/secrecy, that needs targeted support beyond a craving protocol.

chain.node: Low-protein diet diluted by processed carbs and fats
Protein target unmet → protein-specific appetite ('protein leverage') keeps overall food drive switched on until protein is met (FGF21 a proposed mediator, mainly animal data)
Drive to keep eating until protein need is met, overshooting on carbs/calories
Persistent grazing, never feeling truly satisfied, snack cravings

tell.symptoms: You feel hungry again soon after meals, graze all day, and are never quite 'full' — typically on a diet light in protein (skipped/carby breakfast, low-meat/low-dairy days). Cravings ease dramatically on higher-protein days.
tell.labMarker: No lab; estimate dietary protein — chronic intake below ~1.2 g/kg body weight (or <25–30g per meal) makes protein leverage likely. Human evidence supports the leverage effect on total intake; the FGF21 mechanism is largely animal-derived.

fixes.what: Hit 25–40g of protein at each main meal, starting with breakfast
Eat protein first in the meal to reach satiety before excess carbs

chain.node: Frequent ultra-processed foods engineered with sugar+fat+salt
Mesolimbic dopamine reward surge on eating them
Cue-conditioned 'wanting' builds while normal satiety signalling is blunted
Cravings triggered by cues/habit rather than hunger; escalation and 'can't stop once I start'

tell.symptoms: You crave specific hyperpalatable foods even when physically full; cravings are triggered by cues (a screen, the vending machine, a time of day) and it's hard to stop once you start. Portion 'tolerance' has crept up over time.
tell.labMarker: No lab; identified by the cue-driven, reward-seeking pattern rather than a metabolic marker.

fixes.what: Design the environment — remove the trigger foods and cues from home/desk so the conditioned response isn't fired
Reduce ultra-processed food exposure for a few weeks to let reward sensitivity re-normalize
Swap to whole-food versions that satisfy without the engineered reward spike
Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) reduces reward-driven 'food noise'/'wanting' while preserving 'liking' in resistant cases

causes.name: Cyclical hormone shifts (late-luteal / premenstrual cravings)

chain.node: Late-luteal / premenstrual phase (the ~week before a period)
Estrogen and progesterone both fall, withdrawing estrogen's appetite-suppressing effect
Resting metabolic rate runs ~2–6% higher, raising genuine energy demand
Serotonin dips; carbohydrate/sweet intake transiently raises it (proposed, contested)
Predictable sweet/carb/chocolate cravings and bigger appetite in the days before menstruation, easing once the period starts

tell.symptoms: Cravings and hunger ramp up in the ~week before your period (chocolate, sweets, starchy comfort food) and reset once bleeding begins. The timing tracks your cycle, not your meals or sleep.
tell.labMarker: No test needed — diagnosis is by charting cravings against cycle day. Cyclic timing with a late-luteal/premenstrual peak is the tell; daily energy intake rises on the order of 200–500 kcal in this window.

fixes.what: Track your cycle so the premenstrual craving window is expected rather than read as a willpower failure
Eat to the higher luteal energy need with protein + fiber + slow carbs rather than restricting (restriction amplifies the crash-and-crave)
Magnesium — some evidence it eases premenstrual cravings and mood symptoms

causes.confusedWith: PMDD — if premenstrual mood/behavioral symptoms are severe and disabling, that's a distinct condition needing targeted treatment.

ladder: Cold, tired, gaining weight, dry skin, foggy
Too little active thyroid hormone (T3) is reaching your cells
Either the gland can't make enough T4, or your body can't convert T4 into active T3
Missing raw materials (iodine, selenium, iron), an autoimmune attack on the gland, or too little food and too much stress
thyroid.intro: Feeling cold, slow, and puffy when everyone else feels fine is what it looks like when your cells stop getting enough active thyroid hormone (T3) — the master dial on your metabolic rate. But "slow thyroid" is not one disease with one fix. In one person the gland itself is under quiet autoimmune attack; in another the gland is fine but starved of the iodine it needs as raw material; in a third the gland makes plenty of the storage hormone T4 but the body converts less of it to active T3 because selenium, iron, or calories are short. These produce nearly identical symptoms yet need opposite fixes — and a "normal TSH" can hide several of them. The job below is to help you find YOUR driver, because chasing the wrong one wastes months.

causes.name: Hashimoto's autoimmune thyroiditis

chain.node: Genetic + environmental autoimmune trigger
Anti-TPO / anti-thyroglobulin antibodies + cytotoxic T-cell attack
Lymphocytic infiltration, fibrosis and gradual destruction of thyroid follicles
Falling T4/T3 output, rising TSH as the pituitary shouts louder
Cold, fatigue, weight gain, dry skin, hair thinning

tell.symptoms: Symptoms creep in over months to years; often a personal or family history of autoimmune disease (celiac, type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, RA); gland may feel enlarged (goiter) early then shrink; symptoms fluctuate.
tell.labMarker: TPO antibodies positive (assay-dependent, typically >35 IU/mL) is the confirming test; usually with TSH high and free T4 low or low-normal.

fixes.what: Levothyroxine (T4) replacement titrated to TSH, added T3 if conversion is poor
Selenium 100-200 mcg/day can lower TPO antibody titres and supports the gland's antioxidant defence (antibody drop is real; symptom benefit is modest/inconsistent)
Screen for and treat coexisting celiac disease; trial gluten removal if antibodies stay high
Correct low vitamin D and the nutrient deficits (iron, zinc) that often ride alongside autoimmune thyroiditis

causes.confusedWith: Transient/postpartum thyroiditis, which can cause the same picture but self-resolves and is antibody-variable

causes.name: Iodine deficiency (or excess)

chain.node: Low dietary iodine (no iodized salt, little seafood/dairy/eggs) or, conversely, iodine excess
Not enough iodide substrate for TPO to iodinate thyroglobulin and build hormone
Thyroid enlarges (goiter) trying to trap scarce iodine
Low T4/T3, sluggish metabolic rate

tell.symptoms: Diet genuinely low in iodized salt, seafood, dairy and eggs; pregnancy/breastfeeding raises the requirement; visible or palpable goiter. Note the mirror-image: chronic iodine EXCESS (kelp/iodine megadoses) can also suppress the gland.
tell.labMarker: Low urinary iodine concentration (population median <100 µg/L signals deficiency) with elevated TSH.

fixes.what: Use iodized salt as the default salt at home
Eat seafood, dairy and eggs; seaweed occasionally but not daily (kelp overshoots badly)
A modest iodine supplement (~150 mcg) only if intake is truly low — do not megadose, and pair with selenium

causes.confusedWith: Iodine-EXCESS hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) — more iodine makes this one worse, not better

causes.name: Selenium deficiency — reduced T4-to-T3 conversion

chain.node: Low selenium intake (selenium-poor soils, low brazil-nut/seafood/organ-meat diet)
Selenoprotein deiodinases (DIO1/DIO2) under-function; glutathione peroxidase falls
Peripheral conversion of storage T4 into active T3 slips; free T4 accumulates and the free-T4-to-free-T3 ratio rises
May add to sluggish symptoms — but free T3 and TSH often stay normal, and isolated selenium-deficiency hypothyroidism is uncommon where soils and diets are selenium-replete

tell.symptoms: Persistent cold/fatigue/weight symptoms even though TSH and total/free T4 read 'normal'; most relevant with a genuinely selenium-poor diet, and often overlaps Hashimoto's (selenium also blunts TPO antibodies).
tell.labMarker: Low serum/plasma selenium; a raised free-T4-to-free-T3 ratio is suggestive (but nonspecific) of impaired conversion.

fixes.what: 1-2 brazil nuts daily, or seafood/organ meat, as a food-first selenium source
Selenium 100-200 mcg/day (selenomethionine); do not exceed ~400 mcg — excess is toxic
Ensure adequate zinc, a cofactor for deiodinase activity and thyroid receptor function

causes.confusedWith: Overt primary hypothyroidism — here TSH is often normal because gland output (T4) is fine; the shortfall is downstream conversion

causes.name: Iron / ferritin deficiency — impaired TPO synthesis

chain.node: Low iron stores (heavy menstrual loss, low intake, poor absorption/low stomach acid)
Heme-dependent thyroid peroxidase loses activity without iron
Incomplete iodination of thyroglobulin inside the thyroid follicle
Reduced hormone synthesis; fatigue compounded by anemia

tell.symptoms: Most common in menstruating women and vegetarians; fatigue plus hair shedding, pallor, breathlessness on exertion, cold hands; symptoms improve when iron is repleted.
tell.labMarker: Low ferritin (<30 ng/mL suggests depletion, <15 deficient); TSH may drift upward as stores fall.

fixes.what: Iron-rich foods (red meat, legumes) paired with vitamin C to boost absorption; separate from tea/coffee/calcium
Oral iron supplement if ferritin is low, rechecked after ~8-12 weeks
Find and treat the blood-loss cause (heavy periods, GI loss) rather than just topping up

causes.confusedWith: Anemia-of-fatigue alone — iron deficiency and hypothyroidism cause near-identical tiredness and often coexist, so test both

causes.name: Under-eating / chronic stress — functional low-T3 (non-thyroidal)

chain.node: Aggressive dieting, very-low-carb/keto, over-training, or chronic psychological stress
Low insulin/leptin signal energy shortage; high cortisol down-regulates activating deiodinases (DIO1/DIO2) and up-regulates inactivating DIO3
T4-to-T3 conversion falls so active T3 drops, while inactive reverse-T3 rises — rT3 is a marker of this shift, not a receptor blocker
Cold, fatigue, low body temperature, stalled weight loss — with a normal TSH

tell.symptoms: Onset tracks a crash diet, keto phase, big calorie/carb cut, or a period of overtraining and high stress; TSH looks normal but you feel hypothyroid; low libido, cold, plateaued.
tell.labMarker: Low free T3 with NORMAL TSH and normal/high free T4; reverse T3 (rT3) may be elevated but is a nonspecific marker, not a diagnosis.

fixes.what: Eat enough total calories; stop chronic aggressive restriction
Reintroduce adequate carbohydrate — as little as ~50 g/day largely prevents the fasting T3 drop — rather than staying zero-carb
Cut over-training, prioritise sleep and stress reduction to lower cortisol
Ensure adequate protein and omega-3 to support recovery

causes.confusedWith: True primary hypothyroidism — check TSH and TPO antibodies before assuming it is purely functional; this one reverses with feeding and rest

thyroid.theOneThing: Get the actual labs before you treat: TSH, free T4 and free T3 PLUS TPO antibodies and ferritin, with iodine status if diet is a concern — because these five drivers look identical but need opposite fixes. A positive TPO antibody means Hashimoto's and warrants medical management, not just supplements. For everyone else the highest-leverage move is to secure the raw materials and cofactors (iodine sufficiency without excess, iron/ferritin, selenium if your diet is genuinely low) and eat enough food and carbohydrate.

ladder: The cuff reading is the last rung, not the cause — it's the pressure your arteries are already under
Proximate cause: too much fluid volume in the pipes AND pipes that won't relax (lost nitric-oxide vasodilation)
Upstream drivers differ per person: sodium-potassium imbalance, insulin resistance, sympathetic/aldosterone overdrive, or age-stiffened arteries
Root behaviours: what you eat (salt vs. potassium, refined carbs), how you sleep and breathe at night, how much you move and drink
blood-pressure.intro: Blood pressure is the number where several different problems all surface as one reading, which is why two people with identical 150/95 cuffs can need completely different fixes. At its core, pressure is set by two things: how much fluid volume your kidneys are holding, and how easily your arteries relax to let that volume through. Much of \"essential\" hypertension involves the kidneys quietly retaining sodium — driven by a salty, low-potassium diet, by insulin resistance, or by the hormone aldosterone — while the artery walls simultaneously lose their nitric-oxide \"relax\" signal and stiffen with age. But a meaningful slice of cases is powered by things you'd never guess from the number alone: undiagnosed sleep apnea hammering your nervous system every night, or an autonomously over-active adrenal gland. The job here is not to memorise all of them — it's to find YOURS, because the lever that fixes a salt-sensitive 30-year-old does almost nothing for a 65-year-old with stiff arteries or a snorer with sleep apnea.

causes.name: Salt-sensitivity: high sodium, low potassium + endothelial nitric-oxide deficit

chain.node: High-sodium, low-potassium diet (processed/restaurant food, little fruit & veg)
Kidney fails to fully excrete the sodium load; distal-tubule NCC keeps reabsorbing salt and water
Expanded blood volume, plus reduced nitric-oxide vasodilation so vessels can't offload the extra volume
Volume-loaded, under-dilated arteries
Elevated blood pressure

tell.symptoms: Readings jump after salty/restaurant meals; puffiness or ankle swelling; you eat few fruits and vegetables; BP responds well when you cut salt and add produce. Salt-sensitivity is more common with age, higher body weight, and in people of African descent.
tell.labMarker: 24-hour urine sodium >~4 g/day flags high intake; a high spot urinary sodium-to-potassium ratio predicts BP better than either alone; serum potassium often low-normal.

fixes.what: Cut processed and restaurant sodium (the >70% of dietary salt that is hidden) toward <2 g sodium/day
Eat a DASH-style plate: 4-6 daily servings of potassium-rich plants (leafy greens, beans, potato, banana) to shift the sodium:potassium ratio
Dietary nitrate to restore nitric-oxide vasodilation (meta-analyses: ~3-5 mmHg systolic drop, larger in hypertensives)
L-citrulline to raise arginine/NO substrate for vasodilation (RCT meta-analyses: ~4-5 mmHg systolic)
Magnesium, which relaxes vascular smooth muscle and modestly lowers BP (~2 mmHg), mainly in deficiency

causes.confusedWith: Primary aldosteronism — also salt-sensitive and volume-driven, but with suppressed renin and a tendency to low potassium; screen if BP is resistant.

causes.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome (hyperinsulinemia)

chain.node: Excess refined carbohydrate/calories, inactivity, visceral fat accumulation
Insulin resistance → compensatory chronically high insulin
Renal insulin signalling stays selectively intact (via IRS2) so high insulin still drives proximal-tubule sodium reabsorption, plus sympathetic activation
Volume expansion + vasoconstriction + vascular smooth-muscle proliferation
Elevated blood pressure

tell.symptoms: Central/belly weight, high triglycerides, low HDL, creeping fasting glucose, skin tags or darkened neck creases (acanthosis), energy crashes after carb meals. BP tends to travel with weight and waist size.
tell.labMarker: Fasting insulin elevated / HOMA-IR >~2; triglyceride-to-HDL ratio elevated; HbA1c or fasting glucose rising; waist circumference above threshold.

fixes.what: Cut added sugar and refined starch; build meals around protein, fibre and non-starchy veg to lower the insulin load
Resistance training + Zone-2 cardio to restore muscle insulin sensitivity
Lose visceral fat — even 5-10% body-weight loss meaningfully lowers BP
Magnesium, often depleted in insulin resistance and involved in insulin signalling
Omega-3 for triglycerides and endothelial function

causes.confusedWith: Simple overweight without true insulin resistance — check fasting insulin/triglycerides rather than assuming.

causes.name: Obstructive sleep apnea & chronic sympathetic overdrive

chain.node: Obstructive sleep apnea, fragmented sleep, or chronic psychological stress
Repeated nighttime hypoxia and arousals trigger surges of sympathetic nerve activity
Sustained catecholamine drive plus secondary aldosterone elevation
Vasoconstriction and BP that fails to dip at night (non-dipping)
Sustained, often drug-resistant, daytime hypertension

tell.symptoms: Loud snoring, witnessed pauses in breathing, daytime sleepiness, morning headaches, overweight or large neck circumference, and BP that resists 2-3 medications. Often the BP won't drop overnight.
tell.labMarker: Home sleep study / apnea-hypopnea index (AHI): >5 mild, >15 moderate, >30 severe; 24-hour ambulatory BP showing a non-dipping nighttime pattern. OSA is present in ~70-80% of resistant hypertension.

fixes.what: Get a sleep study; if OSA confirmed, CPAP (or a mandibular device) modestly lowers BP (~2-3 mmHg, most in adherent patients), especially nighttime readings
Weight loss and side-sleeping to reduce airway collapse
Stop evening alcohol, which relaxes airway muscles and worsens apnea and BP
Daily slow-breathing / down-regulation practice to lower baseline sympathetic tone

causes.confusedWith: White-coat or anxiety-driven readings — confirm with home or 24-hour ambulatory monitoring before assuming true sustained hypertension.

causes.name: Aldosterone / RAAS overactivation (obesity-linked and primary aldosteronism)

chain.node: Visceral adiposity driving aldosterone, or an autonomously over-producing adrenal gland (primary aldosteronism)
Aldosterone excess relative to suppressed renin
ENaC-driven sodium and water reabsorption with potassium wasting in the distal nephron
Volume expansion plus direct vascular and cardiac remodeling/fibrosis
Hard-to-control hypertension

tell.symptoms: BP resistant to 2-3 drugs, hypertension at a young age or unusually severe, muscle cramps or weakness, needing potassium supplements. Note: potassium is often normal, so normal potassium does NOT rule it out.
tell.labMarker: Aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) elevated with suppressed renin is the screening test; primary aldosteronism is found in ~5-10% of all hypertension and up to ~20-30% of resistant cases — and is badly underdiagnosed (screened in only ~1-2% of eligible patients).

fixes.what: Ask to be screened with an aldosterone-to-renin ratio if BP is resistant, early-onset, or comes with low potassium
If confirmed, a mineralocorticoid-receptor blocker (spironolactone/eplerenone) targets the actual driver
Sodium restriction, which amplifies the BP benefit when aldosterone is high
Weight loss to reduce adipose-driven aldosterone secretion

causes.confusedWith: Essential hypertension with low potassium caused by thiazide diuretics — check aldosterone/renin off or accounting for interfering drugs.

causes.name: Arterial stiffness of aging (accelerated by alcohol & inactivity) → isolated systolic hypertension

chain.node: Aging plus accelerants: excess alcohol, physical inactivity, and glycation from high glucose
Elastin degradation and collagen cross-linking in the aortic wall, with falling nitric-oxide availability
Loss of aortic Windkessel cushioning — the aorta can no longer buffer each heartbeat
Wide pulse pressure / isolated systolic hypertension (high systolic, normal-to-low diastolic)

tell.symptoms: Older age; systolic high (e.g. 150s-160s) while diastolic is normal or even low (e.g. 60s-70s); a wide gap between the two numbers; often a regular-drinker or sedentary pattern.
tell.labMarker: Wide pulse pressure on the cuff (>60 mmHg) is the bedside clue; carotid-femoral pulse wave velocity is elevated if measured.

fixes.what: Cut alcohol — there is a dose-dependent rise in BP above ~1-2 drinks/day via sympathetic and RAAS activation
Regular aerobic exercise, which improves arterial compliance and endothelial function
Dietary nitrate to improve vasodilation and reduce arterial stiffness markers
Omega-3 for endothelial function and modest arterial-stiffness benefit

causes.confusedWith: Volume-driven or salt-sensitive hypertension in older adults — pulse pressure width helps distinguish stiffness-dominant cases.

blood-pressure.theOneThing: Shift your sodium-to-potassium ratio: cut hidden processed/restaurant salt AND add 4-6 daily servings of potassium-rich plants (a DASH pattern). It is the single lever that simultaneously reduces fluid volume, restores endothelial vasodilation, and directly counters salt-sensitivity — the mechanism at work in the largest share of people. Then, if BP stays high despite this, screen for the hidden drivers: get a sleep study if you snore, and ask for an aldosterone-to-renin ratio if it's resistant.

ladder: You see high LDL / cholesterol (or high triglycerides) on your lipid panel

The proximate cause is almost always a clearance-vs-production imbalance: your liver's LDL receptors aren't pulling enough LDL out of the blood, or your liver is over-producing triglyceride-rich particles faster than they clear

That imbalance is driven by upstream levers: a saturated-fat / low-fibre diet, insulin resistance from visceral fat and sugar, alcohol, an underactive thyroid, an inherited receptor defect, or the estrogen drop of menopause

Root behaviours: what fat and fibre you eat, how much visceral fat and refined sugar you carry, how much you drink, plus three non-diet checks (thyroid, family history, menopause status) that change the whole approach
cholesterol.intro: High lipids is rarely one thing. The panel itself only tells you the endpoint; the useful question is which lever is driving it, because the fixes diverge sharply. This section walks the ladder from the number on the page down to the actual driver. The single most important early split is pattern-based: an isolated high LDL points toward diet, thyroid or an inherited receptor defect (a clearance problem), whereas high triglycerides with low HDL points toward insulin resistance or alcohol (an overproduction problem). Two blood tests (TSH) and one question (family history of early heart disease) rule the dangerous mimics in or out before you assume it is just food.

causes.name: Saturated-fat-heavy, low-soluble-fibre diet

chain.node: Diet high in saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter, coconut/palm) and low in soluble fibre

High saturated-fat / cholesterol intake down-regulates hepatic LDL-receptor expression (the SREBP2-regulated pathway that normally raises receptor numbers when the liver senses it needs more cholesterol)
Fewer LDL receptors clearing particles, plus little bile-acid loss of cholesterol (soluble fibre normally binds bile and forces the liver to consume cholesterol to replace it)
LDL particles accumulate in the circulation
Elevated LDL-C on the lipid panel

tell.symptoms: No physical symptoms. This is the person whose diet history is heavy in fried/fatty food, cheese, processed meat and refined starch, and light on vegetables, oats, beans and nuts. LDL usually moves meaningfully within 6-12 weeks of changing the diet.
tell.labMarker: LDL-C above ~3.4 mmol/L (130 mg/dL); typically NOT extreme (rarely >4.9) and responds to diet, which distinguishes it from familial hypercholesterolemia

fixes.what: Replace saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter, coconut oil) with unsaturated fats: extra-virgin olive oil, nuts, avocado, oily fish
Add 10-25 g/day soluble fibre (oats/oat bran, legumes, psyllium husk, apples, barley) to pull cholesterol out via bile
Citrus Bergamot polyphenols modestly lower LDL and total cholesterol (up-regulate LDL receptors); effect is real but smaller than a statin
Red Yeast Rice contains monacolin K, which is chemically identical to lovastatin; it genuinely lowers LDL (~15-30%) but dose/purity varies and it carries the same cautions as a statin

causes.confusedWith: Familial hypercholesterolemia: if LDL stays very high (>4.9 mmol/L) despite a genuinely clean diet, it is not just the food

causes.name: Insulin resistance / metabolic dyslipidemia (visceral fat + sugar)

chain.node: Excess refined carbohydrate/sugar and visceral (belly) fat driving chronically high insulin and free-fatty-acid flux
Selective hepatic insulin resistance fails to suppress VLDL assembly, so the liver over-produces large triglyceride-rich VLDL
CETP transfers triglyceride from VLDL onto LDL and HDL; hepatic lipase then remodels these into small dense LDL and depletes HDL
Result is high triglycerides, low HDL, and small dense LDL particles
Atherogenic pattern on panel: high triglycerides, low HDL, elevated non-HDL/ApoB

tell.symptoms: The giveaway is the PATTERN: triglycerides high and HDL low (not just LDL up), plus central adiposity, larger waist, skin tags, sometimes fatty liver on ultrasound. Often LDL-C looks only mildly high but ApoB/non-HDL is worse than it appears.
tell.labMarker: Triglycerides >1.7 mmol/L (150 mg/dL), HDL <1.0 (men)/<1.3 (women) mmol/L, high TG:HDL ratio; fasting glucose/HbA1c/fasting insulin (HOMA-IR) elevated

fixes.what: Cut refined carbohydrate and added sugar (sugary drinks, white rice/bread, desserts): the strongest single lever on triglycerides
Lose visceral fat via calorie deficit plus resistance and zone-2 cardio; even 5-10% weight loss shifts the whole panel
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA), high-dose (2-4 g/day) lowers triglycerides ~20-30%
Berberine upregulates LDL receptors (stabilises LDLR mRNA and lowers PCSK9) and improves insulin sensitivity via AMPK, modestly lowering LDL and triglycerides

causes.confusedWith: Pure dietary high-LDL: that raises LDL without the low-HDL/high-triglyceride signature seen here

causes.name: Alcohol-driven hypertriglyceridemia

chain.node: Regular or heavy alcohol intake (plus the sugar in many drinks and mixers)
Ethanol metabolism raises hepatic NADH and drives de novo lipogenesis and fatty-acid uptake, increasing VLDL-triglyceride output
Acute alcohol also transiently inhibits lipoprotein lipase, slowing clearance of triglyceride-rich particles
Triglyceride-rich VLDL accumulates; HDL often rises rather than falls
Elevated triglycerides on the panel, sometimes markedly, frequently alongside a raised (not low) HDL

tell.symptoms: High triglycerides that track drinking and drop within 2-4 weeks of cutting back. Unlike the insulin-resistance pattern, HDL is often normal or high rather than low. Heavy intake can push triglycerides very high and risk pancreatitis. GGT is frequently raised.
tell.labMarker: Triglycerides elevated (can exceed 5 mmol/L with heavy intake); GGT and MCV often raised; TG falls sharply with abstinence

fixes.what: Reduce or eliminate alcohol; this is often the single fastest lever when triglycerides are the problem
If triglycerides are very high (>5-10 mmol/L), stop alcohol completely to remove pancreatitis risk
Omega-3 (EPA/DHA) for any residual high triglycerides once alcohol is addressed

causes.confusedWith: Metabolic dyslipidemia: both raise triglycerides, but alcohol usually leaves HDL normal or high, and the pattern reverses with abstinence rather than with weight loss

causes.name: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid)

chain.node: Low circulating thyroid hormone (Hashimoto's, iodine issues, post-partum, medication) with rising TSH
Loss of thyroid-hormone drive on LDL-receptor gene expression; elevated TSH additionally up-regulates hepatic PCSK9, which degrades LDL receptors
Fewer functional LDL receptors, reduced LDL clearance (and reduced bile-acid conversion via lower CYP7A1)
LDL and total cholesterol accumulate
Elevated LDL-C / total cholesterol, sometimes strikingly so

tell.symptoms: High cholesterol that seems out of proportion to the diet, PLUS thyroid clues: fatigue, cold intolerance, unexplained weight gain, constipation, dry skin, hair thinning, heavy/irregular periods. Common and routinely missed; every new high-cholesterol workup should include a TSH.
tell.labMarker: TSH elevated (typically >4-5 mIU/L; overt if >10), free T4 low or low-normal; lipids often improve once thyroid is treated

fixes.what: Get TSH and free T4 tested BEFORE starting a statin; treating the thyroid can correct the cholesterol on its own
Levothyroxine to restore a euthyroid state under a doctor; recheck lipids after 6-8 weeks
Ensure adequate iodine and selenium intake if deficient (do not megadose)

causes.confusedWith: Primary/diet-driven hypercholesterolemia: the fix is completely different, so confirm thyroid status first

causes.name: Familial hypercholesterolemia (inherited receptor defect)

chain.node: Inherited pathogenic variant in LDLR (~85-90% of cases), APOB, or PCSK9 (heterozygous ~1 in 250-300)
Defective LDL receptor or its ligand means LDL cannot be efficiently bound and cleared from birth
Lifelong LDL accumulation; cholesterol deposits in tendons (xanthomas) and arterial walls decades early
Very high LDL from a young age and premature atherosclerotic disease

tell.symptoms: LDL that stays very high despite a genuinely good diet; strong family history of early heart attack or stroke (men <55, women <65); tendon xanthomas (thickened Achilles/knuckle tendons) or corneal arcus at a young age.
tell.labMarker: LDL-C persistently >4.9 mmol/L (190 mg/dL) in adults (>4.0/155 in children); genetic testing of LDLR/APOB/PCSK9 confirms

fixes.what: High-intensity statin is first-line and usually essential; diet alone will not normalise FH
Add ezetimibe and/or a PCSK9 inhibitor if the LDL target is not reached on a statin
Cascade-screen first-degree relatives (parents, siblings, children); each has a 50% chance of carrying it
Diet/fibre and Red Yeast Rice help at the margin but are adjuncts, never the whole answer

causes.confusedWith: Diet-driven high LDL: FH persists despite clean eating and needs pharmacotherapy, not just lifestyle

causes.name: Menopausal estrogen decline

chain.node: Loss of estrogen through perimenopause/menopause
Reduced estrogen-receptor-alpha signalling lowers hepatic LDL-receptor expression and shifts hepatic lipid handling
Reduced LDL clearance; LDL, total cholesterol and often Lp(a) rise (partly compounded by menopausal visceral-fat gain and aging)
LDL/total cholesterol climb in a woman whose lipids were previously fine

tell.symptoms: A woman in her late 40s-50s whose LDL and total cholesterol start rising in step with perimenopausal symptoms (cycle changes, hot flushes, sleep disruption), with no big change in her diet. Aging and visceral-fat gain overlap, so the menopause-specific contribution is real but partly confounded.
tell.labMarker: Rising LDL-C and total cholesterol (and sometimes Lp(a)) tracking the menopause transition; elevated FSH confirms menopausal status

fixes.what: Double down on the diet levers (unsaturated-fat swap plus soluble fibre) and resistance training, which blunt the menopausal lipid shift
Soluble fibre and oily fish/omega-3 to support LDL and triglyceride control
Discuss menopausal hormone therapy with a clinician; it can improve the lipid profile, but the decision is individual and MHT is not prescribed for lipids alone
Consider a statin if cardiovascular risk warrants, independent of hormones

causes.confusedWith: Metabolic dyslipidemia: menopause also drives visceral-fat gain, so the two often overlap and both should be addressed

cholesterol.theOneThing: For most people the highest-leverage move is a single dietary swap plus one blood test: replace saturated fat (fried food, fatty/processed meat, butter) with unsaturated fats (olive oil, nuts, oily fish) AND add 10-25 g/day of soluble fibre (oats, legumes, psyllium), which simultaneously restores LDL-receptor clearance and drags cholesterol out through bile, and also improves the insulin-resistance pattern. If your triglycerides are the problem rather than LDL, look hard at sugar and alcohol first. Before assuming it is just diet, get a TSH and know your family history, because a missed thyroid or an inherited receptor defect needs a completely different fix.

brain-fog.intro: Brain fog isn't a diagnosis — it's what your cortex does when neurons can't get three things at once: steady fuel, clean signaling, and overnight waste removal. Take away any one and thinking turns slow, effortful, and unfocused, like reading through frosted glass. The trap is that the fog feels identical no matter what's causing it, but the causes are wildly different person to person: one person's fog is last night's broken sleep, another's is the swing-and-crash 90 minutes after a rice-heavy lunch, a third's is a ferritin of 18 or a B12 that quietly slid low on a plant-based diet, a fourth's is low-grade inflammation left over from a virus — and very often it's chronic stress, low mood, or a nightly antihistamine quietly dulling everything. Worth knowing: the subjective sense of fog tracks stress and mood more tightly than it tracks any objective memory test, so the mind is as common a driver as the metabolism. Most people who stay foggy have two or three of these stacked together. So the job isn't to find THE cause — it's to find YOURS by matching the pattern: when it hits, what makes it worse, and what one lab would confirm it.
ladder: The fog: thinking feels slow, effortful, words and recall don't come
Proximate: your neurons aren't getting steady glucose, clean neurotransmission, or overnight waste-clearance — or attention is being taxed by stress or a sedating drug
Upstream drivers: sleep debt, chronic stress or low mood, blood-sugar swings, low iron/B12, sedating medications/substances, inflammatory cytokines, or low thyroid — usually more than one
Root: your nightly sleep window, your stress and mental load, what and when you eat, your medication list, and untested nutrient/thyroid gaps
brain-fog.theOneThing: Fix sleep first. Protect a consistent 7-9 hour window with a fixed wake time and get bright daylight within an hour of waking. It's the single most common driver of fog, it clears the adenosine and metabolic waste that directly cause the haze, and it silently amplifies every other cause on this list — nothing else you try works well on a sleep-deprived brain.

causes.name: Sleep debt & circadian disruption

chain.node: Short, fragmented, or mistimed sleep
Adenosine accumulates and inhibitory A1 receptors upregulate (sleep pressure), suppressing wake-promoting cholinergic drive
Overnight glymphatic clearance of metabolic waste drops (mechanism robust in animals, still being confirmed in humans — tier 2)
Prefrontal cortex under-fuels and under-fires
Slow processing, poor recall, effortful thinking

tell.symptoms: Fog is worst in the morning or after a bad night, and lifts noticeably after a couple of good nights. You're under ~7h, waking often, or sleeping at irregular times. Loud snoring, witnessed pauses, or unrefreshing sleep despite enough hours points to apnea driving it.
tell.labMarker: No routine blood test; use a sleep tracker for duration/regularity. If snoring or gasping, a sleep study — AHI ≥5 with symptoms confirms obstructive apnea as the driver.

fixes.what: Fixed wake time 7 days/week and a protected 7-9h window; morning daylight within an hour of waking
No caffeine after early afternoon; dim screens and lights in the last hour before bed
Creatine — a single high dose (~0.35 g/kg) can temporarily blunt sleep-deprivation cognitive decline by sustaining brain ATP (emerging, small studies); routine 3-5 g/day is well tolerated
Screen for and treat sleep apnea if you snore or wake unrefreshed

causes.confusedWith: Depression or adult ADHD — both cause fog and both can be driven or mimicked by chronic sleep loss.

causes.name: Chronic stress, anxiety & low mood

chain.node: Ongoing stress, anxiety, depression, or burnout/exhaustion
HPA-axis activation and sustained cortisol; chronically elevated cortisol impairs hippocampal and prefrontal function
Rumination and worry monopolise working memory and attention (high internal cognitive load)
Prefrontal/attention and hippocampal networks run occupied and under-resourced
Can't focus, blank recall, mentally slow and depleted

tell.symptoms: Fog comes bundled with low mood, worry, irritability, or feeling depleted/burned out; it's worse under stress and eases on holidays or good days. A telling clue: when memory is actually tested it's usually largely intact — the felt impairment outruns measurable deficit, which itself points to mood/stress rather than a metabolic or nutrient cause.
tell.labMarker: No confirmatory blood test; screen with PHQ-9 (depression) and GAD-7 (anxiety). Worth ruling out the testable physical causes on this list in parallel, since they stack.

fixes.what: Treat the driver: psychological therapy (CBT) and/or reduce the actual stressor; regular exercise and adequate sleep both dampen HPA-axis overactivity
Cut rumination load — structured worry time, single-tasking, offloading tasks; mindfulness has modest evidence
If a depressive or anxiety disorder is present, evidence-based treatment (therapy ± medication) lifts the cognitive symptoms alongside the mood

causes.confusedWith: The physical causes on this list — stress/mood is identified by pattern, not by exclusion, and it commonly stacks on top of poor sleep and blood-sugar swings rather than replacing them.

causes.name: Blood-sugar swings / post-meal crashes

chain.node: High-glycemic or sugary meal (refined carbs, liquid sugar)
Sharp glucose spike and a large insulin response

The spike-and-fall swing itself — and in susceptible people a reactive dip 60-120 min later — leaves glucose delivery unstable (true reactive hypoglycemia as a discrete disorder is less common than assumed; much of the fog-glucose link in non-diabetics is CGM-associational)
Neurons — which hold no fuel reserve — see unstable glucose supply
Post-meal haze, drowsiness, can't concentrate

tell.symptoms: Fog reliably hits 1-2 hours after high-carb meals (the classic post-lunch crash), comes with drowsiness, shakiness, or sugar cravings, and eating again relieves it. Often paired with an expanding waistline or a family history of type 2 diabetes.
tell.labMarker: HbA1c high-normal (5.4-5.9%), fasting insulin elevated or HOMA-IR high, or a CGM showing sharp post-meal spikes-then-dips.

fixes.what: Eat protein and fiber before carbs; cut refined sugar and all liquid sugar (lower sugar_g, raise fiber_g/protein_g)
10-15 minute walk after meals to blunt the glucose spike
Swap refined carbs for whole, lower-glycemic sources so fuel arrives steadily

causes.confusedWith: Normal drowsiness from a large meal (postprandial somnolence) — the tell is the delayed crash with concentration loss and cravings, not immediate post-meal sleepiness.

causes.name: Nutrient deficiency (iron / B12 / D / omega-3)

chain.node: Low iron/ferritin or B12 (menstrual loss, plant-based diet, PPIs/metformin, poor absorption)
Iron is a required cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase, the rate-limiting enzyme of dopamine synthesis; B12 drives methylation and myelin upkeep
Reduced O2 delivery + impaired neurotransmitter synthesis and nerve conduction
Attention and processing-speed networks under-supplied
